title: Lay personnel
summary: 9084 — magistrates, juries, selection, and role in the justice system.
learningObjectives: Explain role and powers of lay magistrates.
Outline jury selection, qualification, and discharge.
Analyse advantages and disadvantages of lay participation.

simpleExplanation.title: Ordinary citizens in court
simpleExplanation.summary: Magistrates decide most criminal cases; juries determine guilt in Crown Court — both embed community judgment but face bias and consistency concerns.

flashcards.back: Unpaid volunteers; try 95%+ criminal cases — summary and either-way; advised by legally qualified clerk.
Up to 6 months per offence (12 months total), unlimited fine; may commit to Crown Court for sentence.
18–75, on electoral register, resident 5+ years since 13; disqualified if certain criminal records.
Unanimous preferred; judge may accept 10:2 majority after minimum deliberation time.
Democratic legitimacy, community values, local knowledge, check on state power.
Inconsistency, lack of legal training, unrepresentative (age, ethnicity), media influence on juries.

sections.content: **Lay personnel** (1.3.3) completes Paper 1. Balance **democratic participation vs expertise**. **Magistrates** (Justices of the Peace) are local volunteers advised by **justices' clerks** — they handle most criminal cases. **Juries** (12 citizens) decide guilt in Crown Court trials. Know **selection** (electoral roll, vetting), **challenges**, and **discharge**. Evaluation must address **diversity** (Auld Review, *R v Bow*), **competence**, and **cost** of jury trials.

sections.question: Evaluate the advantages and disadvantages of using lay magistrates rather than professional district judges in the criminal justice system. [15 marks — essay outline]
sections.solution:
**Introduction:** Lay magistrates are central to criminal justice — over 90% of cases; debate centres on **democracy vs expertise**.

**Advantages:** (1) **Community representation** — local knowledge, reflects social values (*Baldwin & McConville* research on community sentencing). (2) **Cost** — unpaid volunteers save significant public expense vs salaried judges. (3) **Democratic legitimacy** — citizens participate in justice, not state officials alone. (4) **Full-time employment** — magistrates bring diverse professional backgrounds.

**Disadvantages:** (1) **Inconsistency** — sentencing varies between benches (*Tarling & Morris*). (2) **Middle-class bias** — magistrates historically unrepresentative of defendants (*Auld Report 2001*). (3) **Reliance on clerk** — legal advice essential; risk of **rubber-stamping**. (4) **Training limitations** — 12 days initial training vs career judges.

**District judges comparison:** Professional judges offer **consistency and efficiency** but less community input; used increasingly for heavy caseloads.

**Evaluation:** Lay magistrates remain **cost-effective and democratic** but **diversity and consistency** reforms needed — recruitment drives, sentencing guidelines, greater use of district judges for complex cases.

**Conclusion:** Justified for most summary/either-way work; hybrid system balances participation with professionalism.

**Exam tip:** Always balance both sides with evidence — statistics, reports, and cases.
